seed: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

transcript.content: I don’t want to be a burden on other people. So I never share anything and pretend I’m fine and smile.

It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy load, feeling like you’re alone with this worry. Have you noticed moments when you’ve actually received help or support, or times when sharing made you feel lighter? What evidence do you have that sharing would make you a burden, and what evidence might suggest the opposite? Exploring those moments could help you see a fuller picture.

Yeah, it does feel like a lot sometimes. I guess I can think of a couple of times when I shared what I was going through, and it did help a little. But then I also remember feeling like I was just putting my problems on someone else, like they had enough to deal with already. I don’t know, it’s like I want to open up but then that voice kicks

It sounds like you’re caught between wanting to share and worrying you might be a burden. What makes you think they’re already overwhelmed by your problems? Have you noticed any moments when opening up actually helped them feel more connected or supported?

It feels like a lot sometimes. I remember a few times I tried to share what I was feeling, and that helped, but then I also worry I'm just burdening others. It’s like I want to open up, but then that voice in my head tells me to keep it to myself.

It sounds like you’re weighing the idea that sharing might be a burden. What evidence do you have that it actually is? And can you think of moments when sharing felt helpful or didn’t feel like a load for others?

I guess when I think about it, I can’t really point to anything specific that says it is a burden. It’s more of a feeling, you know? Like, I just imagine my friends or family being overwhelmed by my stuff. But then, I do remember a time when I opened up to a friend, and they seemed really supportive, even said it helped them to hear me.

It sounds like you’re noticing a mix of worries and supportive moments. What thoughts come to mind when you picture your friends or family feeling overwhelmed by your situation? And what evidence do you have that supports or contradicts that idea?

I guess it’s just a feeling, you know? I can't point to anything specific that says I'm being a burden, but I worry my friends or family might feel overwhelmed by my stuff. I remember opening up to a friend once, and they seemed really supportive, but then I start second-guessing myself about it.

It sounds like you’re carrying a lot of worry about how others might feel, even when you’ve had supportive moments. What evidence do you have that your friends or family actually feel overwhelmed by what you share? Can you recall any times when they seemed relaxed or even appreciative of your openness? Thinking through those moments might help you see whether the fear of being a burden is truly grounded in what’s happening.

Yeah, it’s true. I can’t really say for sure that they feel overwhelmed; it’s more just this nagging fear in my head. I remember a few times when they reacted positively, like they appreciated that I was being honest and real. But then I start to doubt, thinking maybe they were just being nice or didn’t really mean it. It’s confusing.

It sounds like you’re feeling uncertain about how others see you, and that can be unsettling. What evidence do you have that they were genuinely appreciative versus just being polite? And what evidence might suggest the opposite?

Yeah, it is unsettling. I guess when I think about it, maybe they really did care. Like, they looked me in the eye and really listened. But then I can’t help but remember times when I felt ignored or brushed off too. It’s like I go back and forth, and it just makes me second-guess everything I share.

It sounds like you’re noticing both moments of connection and moments of feeling brushed off. What specific things do you remember that made you feel cared for, and what about the times you felt ignored? How might you balance those memories to get a fuller picture of the interaction?

It’s unsettling. I mean, maybe they really did care when they listened to me, and I felt that connection. But then I think about times when I felt ignored or brushed off, and it just makes me doubt everything I share. It’s like I’m stuck going back and forth in my head.

It sounds like you’re caught between feeling heard and feeling dismissed. What moments make you think they truly cared, and what moments make you feel brushed off? How do you know which one is more accurate?

Yeah, that feels right. I think about times when they asked questions and actually seemed interested in what I was saying, like they genuinely wanted to understand. But then there are other times when they were distracted or just didn’t respond the way I hoped, and it felt like I was talking to a wall. It’s hard to know which memories are more real, especially when I’m feeling vulnerable.

It sounds like you’re caught between feeling heard and feeling dismissed, and that mix can make it hard to trust your own memories. What evidence do you have that they were truly uninterested, and what evidence might suggest they were just distracted or preoccupied? Thinking through both sides can help you see whether the “talking‑to‑a‑wall” feeling is the whole story or just one piece of a larger picture.

Yeah, I guess there are times when I felt like they were distracted because they had other things going on, like work or their own stuff. But when I think about it more, there were also moments where they really engaged and asked questions, like they wanted to be there for me. It’s just so hard to weigh those moments against the times when I felt ignored, like it’s always lingering

It sounds like you’re noticing both moments of connection and moments of feeling overlooked. What do you think might be the common thread between those times? How might you tell yourself when you’re feeling ignored that there were also moments of engagement?

Sometimes I feel like they’re distracted, like they have their own stuff to deal with. But then I remember moments when they really engaged with me, asking questions and seeming interested. It’s just tough to balance those memories with the times I felt ignored; it sticks with me.
